frameworks.name: interaction model (similarity-dependent distracter modulation)
frameworks.one_line: distracter influence on choice depends on target-distracter similarity

frameworks.name: independent influence model
frameworks.one_line: target and distracter contribute independently to the decision

frameworks.name: divisive normalization
frameworks.one_line: target features normalized by expectation and variability of local context

frameworks.name: psychophysical reverse correlation
frameworks.one_line: method-level lens used to characterize how features drive choice

core_question: How do irrelevant distracters modulate fine perceptual decisions about a target?
key_assumptions: decisions reflect a weighted combination of target and distracter features
distracter effects can operate at sensory or decision stages
fine orientation judgment indexes sensitivity to target tilt
manipulations: consistency between target and distracter orientation
frame of reference (sensory signal vs decision value)
key_findings: decisions were more sensitive when target and distracter were consistent than inconsistent
the consistency bias acted on decision values, not on sensory signals
the effect was independent of spatial attention
a normalization model using local-context expectation and variability fit the data
interpretations: distracters modulate choice in a similarity-dependent (interaction) manner rather than independently
context-based normalization at the decision stage explains distracter effects
